narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on academic and background factors from past students. The model estimated an 82% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which is high enough to trigger an automatic rejection of your admission to protect the school's outcomes. This decision hinged primarily on your early academic performance in school, specifically your low rankings in Year 1 and Year 3, where you placed in the bottom decile—far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing. Your Year 3 decile rank stood out as the strongest factor pushing the prediction toward failure, as it signals potential challenges in sustaining strong grades over time. Similarly, your Year 1 decile rank reinforced this concern, indicating early struggles that the model sees as a red flag for bar success. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, which is right around the average for applicants, nudged the prediction slightly toward passing, showing some resilience in your college work. Your LSAT score of 35, a bit below the average of 36.7, and middle family income bracket played smaller roles compared to those early deciles. As a full-time student like most applicants, that didn't sway things much either. Overall, your profile compares to typical students by having solid but not standout college stats, yet those bottom-decile school rankings set you apart in a way the model weighs heavily against bar passage. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your school decile rankings, perhaps through targeted tutoring or summer sessions, and aim to improve your LSAT for a stronger reapplication. Building a narrative of academic turnaround, like recent grade improvements, could also help in appeals or future cycles. You're close in some areas—keep pushing, as many students overcome early hurdles with focused effort.
